schools.summary: Not 'apostle' (a claim to authority) but 'a prisoner of Jesus Christ' (desmios, an appeal to sympathy, the chains a silent argument; Paul writing with his manacled hand, Robertson); Philemon 'dearly beloved' and a 'fellowlabourer' (a partner, not a subordinate); affection, not authority, setting the tone.
Chrysostom: calling himself a prisoner strikes Philemon with compunction and quenches anger from the outset.

themes: Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ

schools.summary: The address widened — 'Apphia' (generally Philemon's wife, named next to him as closely concerned in the domestic matter), 'Archippus' (a 'fellowsoldier,' perhaps his son and a minister, Col 4:17), and 'the church in thy house' (the congregation meeting in his home); the personal plea laid before the family and church, adding weight without coercion.

The customary salutation even to this private letter — 'grace to you, and peace,' the same as in the weightier epistles (Rom 1:7; Eph 1:2); the little letter yet framed like the larger ones (Gill); grace and peace jointly 'from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ,' the Father and Son named together, an implicit testimony to Christ's deity.

themes: from the Father and the Son

schools.summary: Before any request, thanksgiving and prayer — Paul does not rush to ask but first thanks God, praises Philemon's graces, and shows his love (Chrysostom), winning the heart before the appeal; 'making mention of thee always' (habitually naming him before God, as the churches); he prays for the very thing for which he gives thanks (Calvin), grace already given still needing increase.

themes: mention of thee in my prayers

schools.summary: The ground of the thanksgiving — Paul had 'heard' (through Epaphras, Col 1:8, perhaps Onesimus) of Philemon's 'love and faith,' which together comprise the whole perfection of a Christian (faith to Christ, love to neighbours, Calvin); the clause 'toward the Lord Jesus and toward all saints' commonly distributed — faith toward Christ, love toward the saints; a man of such love sure to extend it to Onesimus.

The substance of the prayer (not praise but a blessing asked, Calvin) — that the 'communication/fellowship of thy faith' (koinonia, the active sharing and outworking of it) may become 'effectual' (energes, energetic, fruitful) 'by the acknowledging of every good thing in you in Christ' (the full recognition of all faith produces); the fellowship of faith the works of love faith enables (Clarke), preparing the appeal for Onesimus.

Paul's joy in Philemon's charity — 'great joy and consolation in thy love' (gladness at the good report); 'the bowels of the saints are refreshed by thee' (the hearts of God's people revived and given rest by his kindness; 'refresh' the very word of Christ's 'I will give you rest,' Ellicott); the tender 'brother'; preparing the plea — the man who refreshed many to refresh one more (echoed in v20).

themes: the bowels of the saints refreshed

schools.summary: Authority forborne — 'wherefore' (on the ground of Philemon's love and faith) 'though I might be much bold in Christ to enjoin thee' (as an apostle he could rightly command 'that which is convenient,' to anekon, what is fitting), he declines to use this right; the tenderness admired (Paley); forgoing command leaves Philemon free, a freely-granted favour worth more than extorted compliance.

themes: though I might be much bold
to enjoin that which is convenient

schools.summary: The way of entreaty — 'for love's sake I rather beseech thee' (rather than command; we command the unwilling but entreat those who love us, Calvin); two affecting self-descriptions to move Philemon — 'Paul the aged' (presbytes, an old man worthy of reverence, perhaps near sixty) and 'a prisoner of Jesus Christ' (his chains a claim on sympathy); love, age, and bonds pleading together.

The plea named at last — the name 'Onesimus' withheld till Philemon's interest is doubly engaged (Ellicott), then wrapped in endearing titles; 'my son' (his spiritual child, a name given elsewhere only to Timothy and Titus, not 'your runaway slave'), 'whom I have begotten in my bonds' (converted under Paul's prison ministry, 1 Cor 4:15); the depth of his condescension owning a despised slave as his son (Calvin).

themes: I beseech thee for my son

schools.summary: A play on the name — 'Onesimus' means 'profitable/useful'; once 'unprofitable' (achreston, useless and worse, the runaway who wronged his master) but 'now profitable to thee and to me' (converted, answering his name at last); Paul turning his past faults, with a kindly pun, into the background against which his transformation shines; the play oddly unnoticed by the Greek fathers (Ellicott).

The sending back — 'whom I have sent again' (anepempsa; justice and conscience required it, the gospel not cancelling civil relations, the converted slave still legally his master's, Clarke); 'receive him, that is, mine own bowels' (Paul's very heart, dear as his own soul; to receive the slave is to receive Paul); to refuse him cruelty against Paul's own heart (Calvin).

Paul's wish to keep him — 'I would have retained' (eboulomen, imperfect, a real but checked desire) the converted slave, so useful had he become; 'in thy stead he might have ministered' (Onesimus's service to Paul, in effect Philemon's own, for Philemon as Paul's convert owed him service); 'in the bonds of the gospel' (the imprisoned Paul needing help); commending Onesimus and reminding Philemon of his debt.

Respect for free consent — 'without thy mind would I do nothing' (choris tes ses gnomes; Paul would keep Onesimus only with Philemon's approval, respecting his right); 'that thy benefit should not be of necessity, but willingly' (lest the favour seem forced, Ellicott; Paul will have Philemon's kindness wholly voluntary); no sacrifice acceptable to God but the freely offered (Calvin, 2 Cor 9:7); leaving him free is itself persuasive.

themes: without thy mind would I do nothing

schools.summary: Providence overruling the flight — not 'ran away' but 'departed/was parted' (a euphemism veiling the offence, hinting a higher hand); the tentative 'perhaps' (not presuming God's secret purpose); 'for a season' (a short while) 'that thou shouldest receive him for ever' (back permanently, and in a better way, a brother for time and eternity); God turning evil done in malice to a good end (Calvin).

themes: perhaps he departed for a season

schools.summary: The new relation — 'not now as a servant' (no longer merely a slave; conversion not dissolving the civil bond but adding something higher) 'but above a servant, a brother beloved' (raised by grace to spiritual equality); beloved 'specially to me, but how much more unto thee, both in the flesh' (a faithful servant) 'and in the Lord' (a fellow-believer); the brotherhood in Christ the principle that must undo slavery (Ellicott), the seed planted, not emancipation commanded.

themes: in the flesh and in the Lord

schools.summary: The height of the appeal — only after all the preparation, the heart won, does Paul introduce the great request (Chrysostom); 'if thou count me a partner' (koinonon, an associate sharing faith and affection, assumed true), 'receive him as myself' (welcome the slave exactly as Paul in person, identifying himself wholly with Onesimus); surpassing delicacy and tact (Robertson); Paul standing in the runaway's room (Calvin).

themes: if thou count me a partner

schools.summary: Paul assumes the debt — 'if he hath wronged thee, or oweth thee ought' (a delicate hypothetical veiling a likely real wrong; the runaway had probably robbed his master before fleeing); 'put that on mine account' (charge it to Paul, who makes himself surety, undertaking to pay); removing the last obstacle to reconciliation; many seeing a faint echo of Christ bearing the believer's debt.

The signed pledge and gentle reminder — 'I Paul have written it with mine own hand, I will repay' (apotiso, a legal note of hand, the autograph an unusual mark of favour and a sure bond, 2 Thess 3:17); 'albeit I do not say how thou owest me even thine own self' (delicate irony — Philemon owes Paul a far greater debt, his soul, converted under Paul; mentioning the debt by professing not to); how refuse a small debt to one who owes Paul his eternal life?

The final entreaty with a pun — 'let me have joy/profit of thee' (onaimen, a play on Onesimus's name 'profitable'; as Onesimus is to profit Philemon, Paul asks to have profit of Philemon in this matter); 'refresh my bowels in the Lord' (echoing v7, where Philemon refreshed the saints' hearts; now Paul asks his own heart be refreshed by the kind reception of Onesimus), turning Philemon's commended virtue into the thing requested.

themes: let me have joy of thee

schools.summary: Confidence and a hint — 'having confidence in thy obedience' (sure Philemon will grant the request, the trust itself binding him); 'thou wilt also do more than I say' (beyond the explicit request); widely taken as a delicate hint at manumission — that Philemon set Onesimus free, hinted not commanded, left to his own heart (Robertson: Paul 'knows' he will free him but prefers it come freely); consistent with the letter's whole method.

debates.question: Does 'do more than I say' hint at freeing Onesimus?

sides.position: yes — a delicate hint at manumission, that Philemon set Onesimus free, hinted not commanded

schools.summary: The hope of a visit reinforcing the plea — 'prepare me also a lodging' (xenian, a guest-room, Paul content with little); 'I trust that through your prayers I shall be given unto you' (charisthesomai, expecting release as a favour, his coming in God's gift, sought through their intercession); the prospect of Paul's arrival powerfully moving Philemon to deal well with Onesimus (Calvin), a gentle accountability.

The greeting from Epaphras — a Colossian, the minister who led the church at Colosse, well known to Philemon as his townsman, who had come to Rome to seek Paul's help against the false teaching (Col 4:12); 'my fellowprisoner in Christ Jesus' (sharing Paul's confinement for the gospel); Philemon's own pastor joining the appeal, the naming confirming Philemon lived at Colosse.

The fellow-workers named (the same band as Col 4:10-14) — 'Marcus' (John Mark, Barnabas's kinsman, now useful to Paul), 'Aristarchus' (the Thessalonian who shared his dangers), 'Demas' (here a fellow-worker, though he would later forsake Paul, having loved this present world, 2 Tim 4:10, Calvin), 'Lucas' (Luke the beloved physician); all 'fellowlabourers' joining the salute, adding their concurrence to the appeal.

The closing benediction — 'grace' opening (v3) and closing the letter, the free favour of the Lord Jesus, the sum of all blessing (Paul begins and ends with it, Henry); 'be with your spirit' (plural 'your,' falling on the whole household and church addressed, the inmost soul where grace is most enjoyed); fittingly crowning a letter throughout a model of grace; sealed with 'Amen.'

themes: the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ
